---
title: "GAIA Digital Twin of Soil"
subtitle: "A coupled 90 m soil reanalysis for the Pacific Northwest — GAIA HazLab"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyArrowPatch, FancyBboxPatch
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

C_BLUE = "#2E86AB"; C_GREEN = "#3BB273"; C_ORANGE = "#E84855"
C_PURPLE = "#7B2D8B"; C_GREY = "#6B7280"; C_GOLD = "#F6AE2D"; C_TEAL = "#00B4D8"
plt.rcParams.update({"font.family": "sans-serif", "font.sans-serif": ["DejaVu Sans", "Arial"],
                     "figure.facecolor": "white"})
```

## Mission and the hazard-driven scale

**Mission.** Maintain *a living hydromechanical state of the subsurface* — water table depth, soil
moisture, and near-surface stiffness — at the space and time scales that downstream hazard models need
to anticipate **floods, landslides, and liquefaction**. This is a GAIA digital twin of soil (one of
potentially many digital twins); its end product is a coupled soil *reanalysis*.

The one constraint that governs every design choice: **the target spatial scale is set by the hazard,
not by whatever data happen to be available.** Liquefaction in valley fill, shallow landslides on
hillslopes, and compound flooding all resolve at ~100 m or finer, so the state lives on a **90 m grid
from the start** and every input is placed onto that grid at its own native resolution, update rate,
and uncertainty.

## The framework at a glance

```{python}
#| label: fig-mission
#| fig-cap: "**The GAIA Digital Twin of Soil framework.** Three information streams constrain a
#|   coupled subsurface state through a water budget and a hydromechanical rheology. Only ground
#|   sensors and satellites are *assimilated*; static layers form the prior; validation is held out
#|   and scored at native sensor resolution. The downstream hazard sets the target scale (90 m),
#|   which governs the data structure from the start."
INK = "#1a1a2e"; SUB = "#3a3a4e"
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.5, 6.4))
ax.set_xlim(0, 100); ax.set_ylim(0, 100); ax.axis("off")

def _box(x, y, w, h, title, body, hue, fs_t=10.0, fs_b=8.0):
    ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch((x - w / 2, y - h / 2), w, h,
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.5,rounding_size=2.4",
        linewidth=1.5, edgecolor=hue, facecolor=mcolors.to_rgba(hue, 0.13), zorder=2))
    ax.text(x, y + h / 2 - 2.4, title, ha="center", va="top", fontsize=fs_t,
            fontweight="bold", color=INK, zorder=3)
    ax.text(x, y + h / 2 - 6.2, body, ha="center", va="top", fontsize=fs_b,
            color=SUB, zorder=3, linespacing=1.4)

def _arrow(x0, y0, x1, y1, label=None, color=C_GREY, ls="-", lw=1.7):
    ax.add_patch(FancyArrowPatch((x0, y0), (x1, y1), arrowstyle="-|>",
        mutation_scale=14, linewidth=lw, color=color, linestyle=ls, zorder=1, shrinkA=1, shrinkB=1))
    if label:
        ax.text((x0 + x1) / 2, (y0 + y1) / 2 + 1.6, label, ha="center", va="bottom",
                fontsize=7, color=color, style="italic", zorder=3)

ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch((3, 92.5), 94, 6.5, boxstyle="round,pad=0.3,rounding_size=2",
    linewidth=0, facecolor=mcolors.to_rgba(INK, 0.9), zorder=2))
ax.text(50, 95.8, "Target resolution set by the downstream hazard  —  built on a 90 m grid from the start",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="white", zorder=3)

_box(15, 76, 26, 15, "1  Static layers", "lithology · geology\nsoils · terrain\n(fine, time-invariant)", C_GREEN)
_box(15, 51, 26, 15, "2  Ground sensors", "wells · seismic dv/v · met\nsparse space / dense time\ndirect or proxy", C_BLUE)
_box(15, 26, 26, 15, "3  Satellite products", "SMAP · MERRA-2 · GRACE\ndense space / sparse time\nnative res caps output", C_TEAL)
_box(46, 51, 22, 54, "Assimilation +\nphysical models",
     "\n\nWater budget\nP in · ET, runoff out\ninfiltration + capillary\nrise <-> water table\n\nHydromechanical state\nrheology + petrophysics", C_GREY, fs_t=10.5)
_box(74, 71, 22, 13, "Water table depth", "", C_PURPLE)
_box(74, 51, 22, 13, "Soil moisture", "", C_PURPLE)
_box(74, 31, 22, 13, "Near-surface Vs", "", C_PURPLE)
_box(93, 51, 12, 44, "Hazards", "\nFloods\n\nLandslides\n\nLiquefaction", C_ORANGE)
_box(46, 9, 34, 12, "Held-out validation", "scored at native sensor resolution, kept out of processing", C_GOLD, fs_t=9.5, fs_b=7.6)

_arrow(28.2, 74, 34.8, 60, "prior / envelope", color=C_GREEN)
_arrow(28.2, 51, 34.8, 51, "assimilated", color=C_BLUE)
_arrow(28.2, 28, 34.8, 42, "assimilated", color=C_TEAL)
for sy in (71, 51, 31):
    _arrow(57.2, 51 + (sy - 51) * 0.45, 62.8, sy, color=C_GREY)
    _arrow(85.2, sy, 86.8, 51 + (sy - 51) * 0.35, color=C_PURPLE, lw=1.5)
_arrow(58, 11, 63, 26, "benchmark", color=C_GOLD, ls=(0, (4, 3)))
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Five kinds of information constrain the state:

1. **Static layers** — lithology, geology, soils, and terrain-derived hydrology. High-resolution but
   time-invariant; they set what the subsurface *can* do (the envelope of hydraulic and mechanical
   properties) and form the model prior. How they are aggregated and built to the 90 m grid is the
   subject of the [Structural model](00-structural-model.qmd) chapter.
2. **Ground-based sensors** — hydrologic (wells), geophysical (ambient-noise seismic), and atmospheric
   networks. Sparse in space, dense in time; they sense the state directly or by proxy (e.g. seismic
   dv/v as a moisture and pore-pressure proxy). → [Input data](01-input-data.qmd)
3. **Satellite products** — reprocessed retrievals (soil moisture, terrain, total water storage). Dense
   in space, sparse in time; their native resolution caps the resolution of anything derived from them.
   → [Input data](01-input-data.qmd)
4. **Evaluation data** — held out of processing and scored at the native resolution of the sensor,
   never upsampled to flatter the model. → [Evaluation data](02-evaluation-data.qmd)
5. **Physical models we constrain** — a closed **water budget** and a **hydromechanical state** that
   links moisture and pore pressure to stiffness. → [Physics: hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd) ·
   [hydromechanics](03b-physics-hydromechanics.qmd)

## The three coupled states

The twin carries three co-located subsurface state variables on the shared 90 m grid:

- **Water table depth** — the saturated store; the boundary condition for shallow-landslide pore
  pressure and liquefaction.
- **Soil moisture** — the vadose store; the root-zone saturation that triggers hillslope failure.
- **Near-surface stiffness (Vs)** — the mechanical state consumed by geotechnical hazard models.

They are not independent: a closed water budget exchanges water between the vadose and saturated stores
(recharge ↔ capillary rise) and routes excess downhill, and a petrophysical rheology ties the water
states to the stiffness state — so a change in water produces a measurable change in velocity.

## Static envelope × dynamic driver

Every state variable is built the same way: a **fine 90 m static envelope** (what the ground *can*
hold, where water sits, how stiff it can be — from lithology, soils, and terrain) combined with a
**coarse dynamic driver** (how it varies in time — precipitation, observations), statistically
downscaled to 90 m with a tracked static / dynamic / downscaling uncertainty budget. Fine static layers
supply spatial structure; coarse observations supply the time signal; the two are reconciled at 90 m
and anchored to observations.

## The assimilation principle and operating loop

Only the ground sensors (2) and satellites (3) are **assimilated** — everything else is prior knowledge
or physical modelling. Data enter at their own rates, sensitivities, and uncertainties
([Assimilation](04-assimilation.qmd)); the physical models are updated on a regular cadence; and the
output states are validated against the held-out references (4), always at native sensor resolution
([Soil-state evaluation](05-state-evaluation.qmd)). Driven forward from forecast precipitation
([Forecast](06-forecast.qmd)), the same machinery produces the antecedent state that the downstream
hazard models consume ([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)).

::: {.callout-note}
This documentation is organised as independent, linked chapters. The single-file technical report
remains available on the [landing page](../index.html) as an archived full read.
:::

## The chapters

**The twin** — the core development: what the subsurface is, what drives it, the physics, and the forecast.

| # | Chapter | What it covers |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | [Structural model](00-structural-model.qmd) | The **static layers** — how they are aggregated and built to 90 m; the fine skeleton for soil hydromechanics and the hazards |
| 2 | [Input data](01-input-data.qmd) | Ground sensors and satellite products; the assimilation principle; provenance |
| 3 | [Physics — Hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd) | The **closed water budget**: runoff, interflow, groundwater discharge to the rivers, the snowmelt clock — and its calibration against gauges and wells |
| 4 | [Physics — Hydromechanics](03b-physics-hydromechanics.qmd) | The petrophysical link from water to stiffness and dv/v |
| 5 | [Assimilation](04-assimilation.qmd) | Precision-weighted fusion of sensors, dv/v depth separation, the uncertainty budget |
| 6 | [Forecast](06-forecast.qmd) | Driving the twin forward from AI-forecast rainfall; what the forecast predicts and how skill is scored by lead time |
| 7 | [Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd) | Handoff to landslide (LandLab), liquefaction, and flood models |

**Evaluation** — held out of processing, scored at the native resolution of the sensor.

| Chapter | What it covers |
|---------|----------------|
| [Evaluation data](02-evaluation-data.qmd) | Independent references, held out from processing |
| [Soil-state evaluation](05-state-evaluation.qmd) | How the estimated soil moisture and GWL are scored |

**Appendices** — implementation detail that the core report does not depend on.

| Appendix | What it covers |
|----------|----------------|
| [A · Compute and deployment](A-compute-and-deployment.qmd) | Which AI weather model actually predicts rain; GPU execution on Tillicum; Zarr staging on Kopah |
